P3EDU-001.title: Three in 10 Teachers Use AI Weekly, Saving Six Weeks a Year
P3EDU-001.author: Gallup / Walton Family Foundation
P3EDU-001.excerpt: 60% of K-12 teachers use AI tools for their work. 30% use AI at least weekly; 28% use it monthly or less. Teachers using AI weekly report saving 5.9 hours per week on average — approximately 6 weeks of time annually across a 37.4-week school year. Most-used cases: preparing to teach (37% monthly+), creating worksheets/activities (33%), modifying materials (28%). Majorities report quality improvements ranging 57-74%. 'Educators must invest in using AI tools to earn a dividend.'
P3EDU-001.relevance: Primary anchor for teacher AI usage rates and the 'AI dividend' framing — supports both the adoption thesis and the 'investment required' nuance.
P3EDU-001.credibility: high — Gallup is the canonical US polling org; Walton Family Foundation funded; n>2K teachers

P3EDU-002.title: AI Use in Schools Is Quickly Increasing but Guidance Lags Behind: Findings from the RAND Survey Panels
P3EDU-002.author: RAND Corporation
P3EDU-002.excerpt: Between 2023-24 and 2024-25 school years, the share of K-12 teachers using gen AI for work doubled from 25% to 53%. 80%+ of students reported teachers did not explicitly teach them how to use AI for schoolwork. Adoption uneven across grade levels, subjects, and district type. Pre-K teachers: only 29% used GenAI. Title-I/lower-income districts trail. 'AI use in schools is quickly increasing but guidance lags behind.'
P3EDU-002.relevance: Authoritative source for the YoY doubling of teacher adoption and the persistent policy gap.
P3EDU-002.credibility: high — RAND, peer-reviewed methodology, national sample

P3EDU-003.title: Here's How Teachers Really Feel About the Rise of AI in K-12 Education
P3EDU-003.author: Education Week
P3EDU-003.excerpt: Teacher AI adoption surged from 32% in 2024 to 61% in 2025, a 29 percentage-point jump. 83% of K-12 teachers reported using generative AI in some form. Educators remain divided on whether AI should be used in the classroom at all; concern about critical thinking impact persists. In US, 71% of K-12 teachers lack AI training; 85% feel unprepared. By Fall 2025, 74% of districts will provide AI training.
P3EDU-003.relevance: EdWeek is the primary K-12 trade press; this captures the sentiment polarization core to this industry's identity.
P3EDU-003.credibility: high — EdWeek is the canonical K-12 publication

P3EDU-004.title: MagicSchool — Teacher AI Platform
P3EDU-004.author: MagicSchool / humai.blog summary
P3EDU-004.excerpt: More than 6 million educators use MagicSchool as of Oct 2025 — exceeding the total number of K-12 teachers in the United States. Growth: 1,500 teachers in three weeks at May 2023 launch -> 2M in four months -> 4M by Jan 2024 -> 6M+ by Oct 2025. 94% of teachers using MagicSchool report gaining 7+ hours/week. Pricing: Free, Plus, Enterprise (district-wide).
P3EDU-004.relevance: Strongest single signal that teacher-facing AI tooling has crossed adoption chasm. Also shows the freemium + district-pays-not-teacher model that constrains direct C-side monetization.
P3EDU-004.credibility: medium-high — vendor self-report on counts, but the freemium+international counting explains scale; growth pace independently confirmed by EdSurge, Common Sense reviews

P3EDU-005.title: Brisk Teaching — AI Tools for Teachers
P3EDU-005.author: Brisk / EdTech Index review
P3EDU-005.excerpt: Brisk is the AI education platform helping teachers create materials, give feedback, and adapt instruction inside the Google and Microsoft tools they already use. Achieved a 93% Common Sense Privacy Rating — the highest score among educational AI platforms. Browser-extension model lives inside teachers' existing workflows.
P3EDU-005.relevance: Second-tier evidence on K-12 AI tool maturity and the importance of meeting teachers where they already work (Docs/Slides/Classroom).

P3EDU-006.title: What's New for 2025-26 from Khan Academy Districts
P3EDU-006.author: Khan Academy
P3EDU-006.excerpt: Khanmigo usage jumped from ~68,000 student and teacher users in partner districts in 2023-24 to more than 700,000 in 2024-25, expanding from 45 to more than 380 district partners. Expected to exceed 1M students in 2025-26. Khan Academy CLO called this 'the biggest one-year jump in adoption of an education technology' in her 20-year career. Features added: LMS sync (Canvas/Google Classroom/Schoology), admin Khanmigo, multilingual support, image input.
P3EDU-006.relevance: Concrete adoption-curve evidence at district scale (not just individual teacher signup).
P3EDU-006.credibility: high — first-party data with named district counts

P3EDU-007.title: What the research shows about generative AI in tutoring
P3EDU-007.author: Brookings Institution
P3EDU-007.excerpt: Research on AI tutoring efficacy remains mixed. Khan Academy has not yet tested Khanmigo with an RCT due to expense. Over-reliance on AI during practice can reduce performance on unassisted exams. Risk of unequal access between schools with different infrastructure.
P3EDU-007.relevance: Critical counter-evidence against pure adoption-as-success narrative.
P3EDU-007.credibility: high — Brookings is a top-tier think tank

P3EDU-008.title: How AI Is Transforming the EdTech Job Market: Top Roles, Skills & Trends
P3EDU-008.author: EdTechJobs.io
P3EDU-008.excerpt: In July 2025, 21% of new EdTech job listings explicitly mention AI in title, tags, or description. By sector: Corporate L&D leads at 31%, Higher Ed at 24%, K-12 at 17%. Hot roles: AI/ML Engineer, AI Product Manager, AI Curriculum & Training Specialist, AI Ethics & Policy Analyst, AI Search Optimization Specialist. Named JDs: Principal Engineer AI/ML (Quizlet), AI PM (Noodle), AI PD Fellow (Common Sense Media), Prompt Engineer II (ACT). Required: AI literacy, GenAI tool familiarity, data analysis (SQL/Python/Tableau), ethical implications, domain + AI knowledge.
P3EDU-008.relevance: Single best source for 'AI as JD requirement' in education. Crucially shows Corporate L&D > Higher Ed > K-12 hierarchy in AI hiring intensity.
P3EDU-008.credibility: high — primary aggregation across thousands of job listings; specialized publication

P3EDU-009.title: The Six AI Use Case Families of Instructional Design
P3EDU-009.author: Dr Philippa Hardman
P3EDU-009.excerpt: Six AI use case families: Creative Ideation, Research & Analysis, Data-Driven Insights, Adaptation & Localisation, Content Generation, Intelligent Assistance. Newsletter has 29,000+ subscribers. Designers need research synthesis, data interpretation, cultural adaptation, bot design, and strategic AI thinking. 'AI works best as a copilot for instructional design when you switch models as the job changes.'
P3EDU-009.relevance: The most cited individual voice in the AI-instructional-design space; demonstrates the existence of an engaged AI-native L&D community with paying subscribers.
P3EDU-009.credibility: high — 29K subscribers, OpenAI Edu Advisor, ASU+GSV Woman in AI

P3EDU-010.title: Best AI Tools for Instructional Design (2025)
P3EDU-010.author: Disco / synthesis of 144 ID surveys
P3EDU-010.excerpt: Analysis of 144 instructional designers found 83% are leveraging ChatGPT, with accelerating efficiency ranked as the top benefit. 67% achieve moderate-to-significant time savings. Research shows 80% of professionals in instructional design field are already using AI tools in daily work; 65% began using AI within the past year. Common uses: writing learning objectives aligned with Bloom's Taxonomy, storyboarding, drafting narration scripts, editing instructional content.
P3EDU-010.relevance: Shows instructional designer AI adoption is MUCH higher than K-12 teacher adoption (83% vs 53-60%) and concentrated in real workflow.
P3EDU-010.credibility: medium — vendor-published but cites underlying survey; matches other industry estimates

P3EDU-011.title: New Research: How AI Transforms $400 Billion of Corporate Learning
P3EDU-011.author: Josh Bersin
P3EDU-011.excerpt: Global corporate training market = $400B in annual spending. Paradigm shift from 'training' to 'Dynamic Enablement.' Maturity model: Static -> Scaled -> Integrated -> Dynamic Enablement (AI-native). Companies at Level 4: 10x more likely to lead innovation, 6x more likely to exceed financial targets, 16x better adaptability. L&D role transforming from content curator -> capability strategist. 'Employees adopt AI three times faster than organizational leadership.'
P3EDU-011.relevance: Frames the corporate L&D market scale and the strategic transformation that creates demand for AI-literate L&D professionals.
P3EDU-011.credibility: high — Bersin is the most cited HR/L&D analyst globally

P3EDU-012.title: 2025 Training Industry Report
P3EDU-012.author: Training Magazine
P3EDU-012.excerpt: US training expenditures jumped 4.9% to $102.8 billion in 2025. Large company average training budget: $11.7M. Midsize: $1.6M. Small: $333K. Per-learner spending: $874 (up from $774). 37% of organizations using AI for training (up from 25% in 2024). 75% expect to increase AI spend in next fiscal year. 25% have earmarked AI training as 2026 funding priority. Outside products/services spending: +29% to $16B.
P3EDU-012.relevance: Quantifies the L&D market and the rate of AI absorption — supports the thesis that L&D budgets dwarf K-12 willingness-to-pay and are growing AI-mature.

P3EDU-013.title: AI Learning Agents in Corporate L&D: 2025 Research Analysis
P3EDU-013.author: Continu
P3EDU-013.excerpt: AI learning agents emerging in corporate L&D. Two camps: content-generation focused (faster production, no retention correlation) vs agentic-learning focused (70-80% knowledge retention after 30 days, 2x traditional training). Outcomes: 50-80% reduction in time finding info; 30-60% fewer help-desk tickets; 60-75% faster time-to-competency (8-12 wks -> 2-3 wks); $1.2-3K annual productivity gains/employee. Vendor landscape: Eddy, Harmony (Docebo), Kiki (Thrive), AI Companion (360Learning), CYPHER Agent.
P3EDU-013.relevance: Concrete proof that AI in corporate L&D is operationally deep, not just hype.
P3EDU-013.credibility: medium — vendor-aligned but the vendor list is verifiable and outcomes match other reports

P3EDU-014.title: AI in 2025: The Turning Point for Learning & Development
P3EDU-014.author: Chief Learning Officer Magazine
P3EDU-014.excerpt: CLO role evolving from 'content curator' to 'capability strategist.' L&D professionals must transition from managing libraries to architecting AI-integrated ecosystems. Employees who receive 5+ hours of formal AI training are 12 percentage points more likely to become regular users. Employees adopt AI three times faster than organizational leadership. Pilot programs are the practical entry point.
P3EDU-014.relevance: Authoritative L&D press source for the role-evolution thesis and the demand for AI-fluent L&D pros.
P3EDU-014.credibility: high — CLO Magazine is the established L&D industry publication

P3EDU-015.title: ATD, AI Expert Josh Cavalier Collaborate to Deliver AI Education to Talent Development Professionals
P3EDU-015.author: Association for Talent Development
P3EDU-015.excerpt: ATD launched 'Applying AI in Learning and Development Certificate' — three-day program covering AI strategy, performance support, instructional design, prompt engineering, latest models/tools. Four offerings in 2025. ATD also offers AI for Talent Development Workshop and AI Intensive. 63% of employers cite skill gaps as a major barrier to transformation over next five years.
P3EDU-015.relevance: Institutional confirmation that the L&D profession's gatekeeper body is now codifying AI as required skill.
P3EDU-015.credibility: high — ATD is the primary professional body for L&D in the US

P3EDU-016.title: Educator Pay Data 2026 (NEA)
P3EDU-016.author: National Education Association
P3EDU-016.excerpt: National average full-time teacher salary: $74,200 (2025, +4.1% YoY). Average public school teacher salary rose from $71,985 (2023-24) to $74,495 (2024-25), nominal +3.5%. Range: $46K (Mississippi) to $92K (NY). Starting NYC teacher: $68,902 (BA) / $77,455 (MA).
P3EDU-016.relevance: Anchors the willingness-to-pay analysis. Teachers earn substantially less than instructional designers / corporate L&D peers.
P3EDU-016.credibility: high — NEA is the primary teacher union, canonical pay data

P3EDU-017.title: Instructional Designer Salary 2026 (PayScale)
P3EDU-017.author: PayScale / Glassdoor / Robert Half / Salary.com / Indeed / LinkedIn
P3EDU-017.excerpt: Instructional designer US average: $79,513 (PayScale) - $92,736 (Glassdoor). Robert Half range: $76,750-$122,750. Associate-level: $66,425. Entry (<1 yr): $61,589; 1-4 yrs: $68,246. 2,000+ active LinkedIn openings. BLS projects faster-than-average growth.
P3EDU-017.relevance: Quantifies that instructional designers earn 10-50% more than K-12 teachers and have higher career mobility; this directly supports the 'go after L&D / ID, not classroom teachers' positioning.

P3EDU-018.title: Varsity Tutors Launches Live+AI Platform for Schools
P3EDU-018.author: Nerdy / Varsity Tutors
P3EDU-018.excerpt: Varsity Tutors (Nerdy Inc.) introduced Live+AI platform combining human tutoring with context-aware AI. 40+ AI-powered teacher tools reducing lesson planning + admin by 7-10+ hours/week. AI tutoring services market projected to reach $5,754M by 2035, 15% CAGR.
P3EDU-018.relevance: Tutors are a sub-segment with direct revenue stake in AI workflow mastery; market is sizable and AI-native.
P3EDU-018.credibility: medium — vendor press release; market sizing from FactMR

P3EDU-019.title: AI Has Come for K-12 Education
P3EDU-019.author: American Enterprise Institute
P3EDU-019.excerpt: AEI commentary on the rapid invasion of AI into K-12. Notes the asymmetry: students adopting fast (54% per RAND), teachers catching up, schools' policies lagging. The 'cheating panic' phase has subsided but been replaced by deeper uncertainty about what we're actually teaching.
P3EDU-019.relevance: Conservative think-tank perspective on the disruption — adds ideological balance to the largely positive vendor/teacher-union framing.
P3EDU-019.credibility: medium-high — AEI is a major US think tank; this is commentary not research

P3EDU-020.title: A framework to avoid AI tool fatigue in schools
P3EDU-020.author: SchoolAI / BAM Radio synthesis
P3EDU-020.excerpt: AI tool fatigue affects most educational leaders; constant platform switching contributing to 44% burnout rate among K-12 teachers. For 2023-24 school year, school districts accessed an average of 2,739 distinct edtech tools annually. 76% of educators nationwide affected by burnout. US teachers are 40% more likely to experience anxiety symptoms vs healthcare workers.
P3EDU-020.relevance: Critical counter-pressure: K-12 teachers are saturated with tool-overload, lowering appetite for yet-another-platform like a new identity product.
P3EDU-020.credibility: medium — vendor blog, but burnout stats come from established sources

P3EDU-021.title: Exploring the acceptance of ChatGPT in higher education: a comprehensive quantitative study of university students and faculty
P3EDU-021.author: Frontiers in Education (peer-reviewed)
P3EDU-021.excerpt: 92% of UK higher-ed students use AI in some form (up from 66% in 2024). Faculty behavioral intention scores for ChatGPT high but habit scores consistently low — step-by-step integration rather than daily use. Some lecturers using LLMs as 'double markers' to cross-reference grading.
P3EDU-021.relevance: Higher-ed student adoption is near-saturation; faculty adoption is rapid but lagging behind. Useful for university-tier breakdown.

P3EDU-022.title: AI is running rampant on college campuses
P3EDU-022.author: Fortune
P3EDU-022.excerpt: Mainstream coverage of AI saturation on college campuses; professors largely unable to detect or stop student AI use; many leaning on AI themselves for course design and grading. Tension between policy ban and pragmatic adoption.
P3EDU-022.relevance: Mainstream business-press confirmation of the 'AI is everywhere in college, no consensus on what to do' state.

P3EDU-023.title: Teachers Pay Teachers — AI products by teacher creators
P3EDU-023.author: TPT / multiple creators
P3EDU-023.excerpt: TPT (the dominant teacher creator marketplace, ~7M registered teachers, ~85% of US teachers visit) now hosts thousands of AI-related products from teacher creators: prompt packs, lesson plan generators, AI implementation guides, ChatGPT tutorial bundles. Indicates a TEACHER-CREATOR economy with measurable willingness to sell AI knowledge — but most products priced $1-$15.
P3EDU-023.relevance: Direct evidence that teachers (as creators) ARE monetizing AI expertise, just at low per-unit prices. This is the most plausible direct-monetization model in K-12.
P3EDU-023.credibility: medium — TPT is a real platform; pricing/scale claims are inferred from product browsing

P3EDU-024.title: UNESCO AI Competency Framework for Teachers
P3EDU-024.author: UNESCO
P3EDU-024.excerpt: UNESCO published a global AI Competency Framework for Teachers covering: human-centered mindset, ethics of AI, AI foundations and applications, AI pedagogy, AI for professional development. Five proficiency levels: Acquire, Deepen, Create. Used as reference by multiple national ed ministries.
P3EDU-024.relevance: Institutional codification of what 'AI literacy for teachers' means — gives shape to the otherwise-vague competency.
P3EDU-024.credibility: high — UNESCO, used as policy reference globally

P3EDU-025.title: US DOL Training and Employment Notice 07-25: AI Literacy Framework
P3EDU-025.author: US Department of Labor
P3EDU-025.excerpt: US DOL issued AI Literacy Framework providing voluntary guidance for evaluating and integrating AI literacy content into education and workforce systems. Targets workforce boards, community colleges, K-12 districts, employer training providers.
P3EDU-025.relevance: Government-level codification that AI literacy is a workforce-development priority spanning education AND corporate L&D.

P3EDU-026.title: Devlin Peck Instructional Design Portfolio Showcase
P3EDU-026.author: Devlin Peck
P3EDU-026.excerpt: Curated gallery of ~40+ instructional design portfolios from people who completed Devlin Peck's ID Bootcamp. Organized by 'Recently Hired,' 'On the Market,' 'Freelancers.' One-way showcase (no community/network features). Does NOT focus on AI projects — showcases traditional eLearning, scenario-based design, Articulate Storyline work.
P3EDU-026.relevance: The closest analog to a 'portfolio/identity platform' for instructional designers — and its narrowness (one creator's funnel, ~40 portfolios) reveals the vacuum in this market. No GitHub-equivalent exists for IDs.
P3EDU-026.credibility: medium — single creator's site, but the absence of competitors is itself the finding

P3EDU-027.title: LinkedIn 2025 Workplace Learning Report
P3EDU-027.author: LinkedIn Learning
P3EDU-027.excerpt: 71% of L&D professionals are already exploring, experimenting with, or integrating AI into their work. 36% qualify as 'career development champions'; champions are 42% more likely to be GenAI frontrunners (51% vs 36%). Career progression is the #1 motivator for employees to learn. Nearly half of L&D leaders say executives worry employees lack required skills. 31% YoY growth in postings recognizing verifiable AI certifications (2024->2025).
P3EDU-027.relevance: Most authoritative single source confirming L&D is one of the highest-AI-adoption professions and the segment most likely to value AI-competency signaling.
P3EDU-027.credibility: high — LinkedIn primary research with large global L&D sample

P3EDU-028.title: Regulating AI Isn't Enough. Let's Dismantle the Logic That Put It in Schools.
P3EDU-028.author: Truthout
P3EDU-028.excerpt: 'Permission-without-policy' problem: tools are in buildings, use is already underway, and the guidance that would make that use purposeful and protected is not in place. Teachers receive the message that AI is coming and they are expected to embrace it, often without serious discussion of pedagogy, ethics, or structural inequities AI may deepen.
P3EDU-028.relevance: Critical voice articulating teacher resistance/ambivalence — adds ideological balance to the largely-positive vendor narrative and explains why K-12 is a hostile sub-market for an 'AI identity' product.
P3EDU-028.credibility: medium — opinion-leaning outlet, but captures a documented teacher sentiment

P3EDU-029.title: Why I'm Banning Student AI Use This Year
P3EDU-029.author: Chanea Bond / Edutopia
P3EDU-029.excerpt: Veteran high school English teacher publishes essay explaining her decision to ban student AI use in her classroom for 2025-26 school year, citing concerns about thinking development and authentic assessment. Edutopia (the George Lucas Educational Foundation publication) continues to platform teacher voices on multiple sides of the AI debate.
P3EDU-029.relevance: Concrete example of teacher resistance from within the profession's most respected publication — proves the polarization is genuine, not just media narrative.
P3EDU-029.credibility: medium-high — Edutopia is a major teacher-facing publication
